title: S Corporation Reasonable Compensation Analysis and Optimization

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must determine and pay reasonable compensation to shareholder-employees based on industry standards, duties performed, and multiple factors to optimize tax savings while maintaining IRS audit defense
criteria: Must be an S Corporation shareholder who performs services for the corporation
Shareholder-employee must receive W-2 wages before taking distributions or earnings
Compensation must be reasonable based on duties, qualifications, time spent, and comparable industry standards
Consider multi-factor analysis: training and experience, duties and responsibilities, time and effort, business complexity, market comparisons
Use industry compensation data, salary surveys, and comparable position analysis to support compensation level
Document compensation determination process and rationale for IRS audit protection
Balance between adequate compensation and distribution optimization to maximize overall tax efficiency
Review and adjust compensation annually based on business performance and changing market conditions

reasonable_compensation_factors.irs_scrutiny: IRS actively audits S Corp compensation; inadequate salary leads to reclassification and penalties
reasonable_compensation_factors.safe_harbor_guidance: No bright-line rule; generally 40-60% of net income considered reasonable depending on industry and role
reasonable_compensation_factors.payroll_tax_impact: Higher salary increases payroll taxes but may increase QBI deduction and retirement plan contributions
reasonable_compensation_factors.distribution_optimization: Lower salary increases distribution amount avoiding 15.3% self-employment tax on distributions
reasonable_compensation_factors.multi_factor_test: IRS uses multiple factors including training, duties, time, comparable salaries, and business conditions
reasonable_compensation_factors.industry_benchmarks: Use BLS data, salary surveys, and industry reports to support reasonable compensation determination

restrictions: Unreasonably low compensation subject to reclassification as wages with back payroll taxes, penalties, and interest
Cannot pay zero salary and take only distributions if shareholder provides substantial services
Higher compensation reduces QBI deduction potential (wages reduce qualified business income)
Social Security wage base cap ($168,600 for 2024, $176,100 for 2025) limits Social Security tax on higher salaries
Officer compensation must be reported on Form 1120-S Line 7 and reconciled with W-2 wages
Compensation must be paid through proper payroll with timely tax deposits and quarterly filings
Excessive compensation (opposite extreme) could trigger audit if attempting to maximize corporate deduction
Court cases emphasize reasonable compensation analysis and documentation critical for successful IRS defense
